title: Language speaks more than it says: some reflections on the work of Basil Bernstein
abstract: It seems hard to reflect on a time when language was not a central concern for sociological theory yet when we look back on the late 1950s the period Basil Bernstein was forming his groundbreaking thesis on socio-linguistic codes he stood alone, regarded almost as a maverick. As he once put it ‘Humans speak yet sociology remains silent on the subject’. His subsequent impact on the sociology of education, educational theory and policy was to be monumental as was the growth of his personal reputation and influence. This paper and the one that preceded it in 1958 were crafted some while before he assumed the Karl Mannheim Chair in the Sociology of Education at the University of London Institute of Education in 1967, a position that secured his place in the sociological establishment but a platform from which he also continued to shock and surprise that same establishment with his innovation and irreverence. His early prose is rather more stilted than his more mature, more assertive work and he displays a tendency to employ inventories of points as if to convince by number rather than to recognize, as he later did, that he simply had lots of ideas frequently employed in parallel. Basil often claimed that it took him a while to realize what his real concern was, an evolution in thinking that is familiar to a lot of us. Many of the seeds of his final thesis are revealed in this paper but ironically they are left implicit or spoken in a lower register. There is a charming moment in the final section of the paper where he acknowledges a debt to Sapir, one that I never found entirely clear in his work, and then draws a dichotomy between his public and formal languages as ‘. . . the old polarity of gemeinschaft and gesellschaft in another guise’ (Bernstein 2010 [1959]). Poor Tonnies does not get mentioned by name, is this because his concepts are such familiar elements of the sociological architecture or because Bernstein's real inspiration lies elsewhere? Of course! All of Bernstein's work is an articulation of the relationship between the social structure and the symbolic order and its unfolding over nearly four decades reveals the author to be the true heir of Emile Durkheim, not Tonnies. Surely Durkheim did not address language, well no, he employed different metaphors, he looked at elementary religious practice, categories of knowledge and totemism, and most significantly in his foundational work he set two forms of solidarity, the mechanical and the organic. So here is another Bernsteinian challenge to the going order, by the late 1950s and certainly through the 1960s Durkheim was essential background study, ‘a classical theorist’, a bit of a museum piece for sociology but as an active dimension in research much more the province of anthropology. It was not the case that sociology had entirely thrown off the mantle of structural-functionalism but the American hegemony of ‘we are all functionalists now’ was waning and more individualistic action theories were taking hold, symbolic interactionism staged a comeback and by 1967 Garfinkel had published Studies in Ethnomethodology. The new phenomenologists (me included) were at war with ‘positivism’ and Durkheim was a prime target (with Basil's inspiration I finally came round and integrated Durkheim with my project). Bernstein's work manifested, oozed and championed Durkheim's key ideas. In the Introduction to the first volume of his collected papers Class, Codes and Control (Bernstein 1971–77) Bernstein humorously and rather mischievously says that it never really mattered to him that Durkheim was a ‘naughty functionalist’, his message is too significant to be overwhelmed by fashion or contemporary factionalism. However, at that time to align oneself wholeheartedly with Durkheim and to establish a large SSRC funded research group entirely based on his theoretical canons was at least brave, at worst misguided, and either way very much against the flow of the zeitgeist. So what grounds this paper on ‘Public language’ (Bernstein 2010 [1959]) and the interest in linguistic form? Well, among the stock of anecdotes that Basil would both entertain with and enlighten through there were many about his teaching experience in FE with Post Office trainees and motor mechanics. It was here, he claimed, that he became attuned to the social context regulating the form and expressive power of speech. The lads he worked with made complete sense to one another albeit within a world where much of the culture was taken for granted. Speech here could be coarse, clipped, constrained but at the same time intimate in as much as it paid obeisance to its social context, the world that the lads themselves shared. Their language made reference to what they had in common; their language was a symbolic representation of the form of their relationship. This was an eloquent and subtle realization of Durkheim's mechanical solidarity, a society where the division of labour is based on sameness not differentiation. Within such a world the symbols are condensed and highly predictable, the meanings tend to be particularistic, the roles of individuals are based on consensus and thus assigned, and a major consequence of such a configuration is that there is a voluntary restriction placed on a person's ability to express their unique intent. At our most sentimental (and Basil could do schmaltz) we might claim that this is precisely the kind of relationship we all treasure, that we long for, the language of lovers or close friends. At our most rational however we must attend to the fact that such language is inward looking, self-referential and constraining; it does not speak to or learn from others. Where such language users encounter the wider world, most critically through socialization in the form of education, they are placed at a disadvantage. Schools thus provide a discontinuity in the experience of children nurtured in this form of social bond, they are distanced or alienated from the dominant message system, and they demonstrate what we then called low educability. Bernstein had thus established both the poetics and the politics of his phenomenon and so the work grew, exercising both dimensions. Early on in his paper Bernstein states that . . . a public language was discussed with reference to its use by the unskilled and semi-skilled strata, but approximations to a public language may well be spoken in such widely separated groups as criminal sub-cultures, rural groups, armed forces and adolescent groups in particular situations. (Bernstein 2010 [1959]: 53 [311]) which was his attempt to broaden the scope and the applicability of the concept and also his desire to free the idea from a classist and hierarchical designation that had been readily attached by his critics, like Coulthard (1969) and Rosen (1973), and which persisted in some quarters for the duration –Labov (1966) even interpreted it as a theoretical justification for racism! On the face of it the examples he chose were not particularly value-free –‘lumpen proles, bumpkins, squaddies and teddy boys!’ and subsequent references to the Elephant and Castle; the Angel, Islington; the Gorbals and Tiger Bay did not really help. These were not politically correct times but there was a powerful Marxist lobby within the academy which sought out any taint of social or cultural stratification in the arts, humanities or social sciences and Basil always lit the touchpaper, sometimes provocatively. In reality such appropriations and accusations hurt him and could not have been further from his actual intentions. Though far more sophisticated than the coal-in-the-bath causalities generated by some of his contemporaries Bernstein was nevertheless part of the very influential movement in sociology and social policy that addressed the systematic ‘wastage of talent’ in the English educational system. The mantra ran that although intellectual ability is randomly distributed educational ability is socially distributed. Bernstein was actually outraged by the inequalities and indignities visited upon the educational experience of working class children, he railed against such processes and the methodologies and conventions that enabled their reproduction. He was himself the son of an immigrant Jewish family brought up in the East End of London where he was later to work in a settlement for deprived children; for him the injuries inflicted by the class system and the anger and resentment that this generated were a first hand experience. This paper was written only 15 years after the Butler Act which, for the first time in this country, established free secondary education for all. The intellect behind this paper was later to contribute to the Newsom Report of 1963 that addressed the relative neglect of at least ‘half our future’. Here was a man who moved from high level theory to policy formation. ‘Language . . . ’ Bernstein asserts ‘. . . is considered one of the most important means of initiating, synthesising, and reinforcing ways of thinking, feeling and behaviour which are functionally related to the social group’ (Bernstein 2010 [1959]: 54 [312]) and this is a fairly comprehensive gathering of the scope of social action around a single source. He is not saying, as we might today, that language brings the world into being, but he is saying in true Durkheimian mode that the first categories of understanding are categories of relationality. This is the neo-Kantian thesis espoused in The Elementary Forms of Religious Life (Durkheim 1971) and Primitive Classification (Durkheim and Mauss 1963). However at this stage the philosophical import of Bernstein's ideas are masked by the minutiae of his then prevalent socio-linguistic methodology – describe a type, give an example, and add it to the list with an almost compulsive drive to saturate the phenomenon by reference to an implicit quantitative rule. Who else could contrive to write ‘Finally, the tenth and most important characteristic may be regarded as the determinant of the previous nine’! (Bernstein 2010 [1959]: 57 [315]). Probably this paper is more important for what it instigated than for what it said yet, I repeat, at this time nobody else regarded language as a social phenomenon and Bernstein was probably lucky to get it published in this journal. It was clearly pioneering of the BJS to support the work, I wonder who the peer reviewers were? Numerous other papers followed and the work attained a hybrid acceptance as mainstream yet controversial. Although this disappointed Basil by never quite rewarding him with the significance he both desired and deserved it also perfectly suited his predisposition to unpredictability. As a self-designated ‘old fashioned Freudian hysteric’ he swung; thus theoretically, pedagogically or interactionally he could be charming, waspish, supportive, undermining, consensual, shocking but rarely less than inspirational and sometimes all within the space of the same encounter. His volatility kept you alert and his passion and enthusiasm made you realize that there could be no better worldview than that of the sociologist. His overwhelming skills were linguistic, he could work an audience, he lived his thesis to perfection. The idea of different ‘languages’ that this paper began took on different names and finally morphed into his most famous concept, that of ‘codes’. Bernstein's emergent theory states that there are crucial qualitative differences between working-class and middle-class patterns of speech. These differences are enduring, they are socially divisive and thus they are politically significant. The differences are a matter of content rather than form or structure. So Bernstein is not manifestly concerned with syntax or dialect or slang, he is concerned with the different uses that these grammatical and lexical forms are put to in social contexts. In order to convey a sense of this more fundamental level of communication he invokes the concept of a code which is meant to describe a link between a form of social experience and its realization in a social context. The two social classes that he typifies are said to be operating with different codes because there are differences in the principles which underlie the particular choices they make in speech. Codes are not meant to refer to the language used but rather to the different planning procedures out of which the choice of language emanates. The two different codes, which we now know as the restricted and the elaborated are seen as representations of different forms of social relation that exist within working-class and middle-class communities (and they correspond to Durkheim's mechanical and organic forms of the division of labour). These forms of social relation are realized through different control variants, the positional and the personal, that the different class groups employ in their socialization practices. At school children socialized within the positional variant come into collision with the linguistic form of pedagogy which is elaborated and as Bernstein tells us the elaborated code is that form of experience which gives rise to the principles of intellectual change and innovation (Bernstein 2010 [1959]). The consequences of this collision are predictable and replicable. Even though Bernstein believed in education as a vehicle for social change the fateful and depressing backdrop to his thesis is that education cannot compensate for society, a fact that has come back into prominence after successive waves of governmental social engineering. We may well achieve mobility at the margins but if a social structure is founded on social stratification and depends on that stratification in order to function then it will persist. Neither is it the case that each generation starts off at square one in a meritocracy. The resounding power of Bernstein's work is that he successfully demonstrates the constraints on working class achievement are not simply material (as if relative poverty were not enough), they are cognitive and linguistic. Now boys and girls, open your books at the beginning please . . . .
